title: Functions - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Code-based logic that takes input parameters and returns output, natively integrated with the Ontology. Functions include first-class support for reading properties, traversing links, and making Ontology edits. Executed server-side in an isolated environment.

extractedEntities.name: Query
extractedEntities.description: A read-only subset of Functions exposed through the API gateway with no side effects. Queries cannot modify Ontology state and are safe for external consumption.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects that can be returned by Functions for use in applications like Workshop dashboards.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A semantic type in the Ontology whose properties can be read and links traversed by Functions.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology entity that can be backed by Functions to express complex edits updating many objects.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A typed attribute of an Object Type whose values can be read and transformed by Functions.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A defined relationship between Object Types that Functions can traverse to navigate the Ontology graph.

extractedEntities.name: External Function
extractedEntities.description: A Function variant that queries external systems to enrich objects in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application for building dashboards and operational applications. Functions return object sets and values for use in Workshop, including function-backed columns and charts.

extractedEntities.name: Slate
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application where Functions run backend logic to return information displayed in the frontend.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Palantir analytics application where Functions compute custom metrics or aggregations for display.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Palantir data transformation application where Python functions can be used as sidecar containers.

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript Functions
extractedEntities.description: Functions can be authored in TypeScript, with v1 and v2 language versions supported.

extractedEntities.name: Python Functions
extractedEntities.description: Functions can be authored in Python, supporting use cases including sidecar containers in Pipeline Builder.

keyInsights: Functions are the primary mechanism for embedding code-based business logic into the Ontology, bridging data and action.
Queries are a read-only, side-effect-free subset of Functions specifically designed for safe external API exposure.
Functions provide first-class Ontology integration: reading properties, traversing links, and making edits — not just arbitrary code execution.
Function-backed Actions enable complex multi-object edits expressed as code rather than declarative rules.
External Functions extend the Ontology beyond Foundry by querying external systems to enrich object data.
Functions execute server-side in isolated environments, ensuring security and resource control.

title: Ontology SDK - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Software Development Kit that provides type-safe, generated code for accessing the Ontology from external development environments. Supports TypeScript (NPM), Python (Pip/Conda), Java (Maven), and OpenAPI for other languages.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application for creating and managing OSDK applications, including bootstrapping and configuring Ontology access.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that serves as the backend for OSDK applications, providing high-scale queries, writeback, and granular governance controls.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A semantic type in the Ontology for which the OSDK generates strongly-typed interfaces and query functions.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attributes whose names and descriptions are embedded in OSDK-generated code as metadata available in the editor.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types that are represented as typed accessors in the generated OSDK code.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology actions that are exposed as type-safe functions in the generated OSDK code for performing writebacks.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology entity supported by the OSDK for polymorphic access to Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Code Generation
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK generates strongly-typed code from Ontology metadata, scaling linearly with Ontology shape rather than contents. Supports lazy loading.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK 2.0 Scaling
extractedEntities.description: OSDK 2.0 scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata rather than actual Ontology contents, and supports lazy loading of generated types.

extractedEntities.name: Scoped Token
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK uses a token scoped only to the ontological entities the application needs to access, combined with the user's own data permissions.

keyInsights: The OSDK treats Foundry as a backend, enabling external applications to leverage Ontology queries and writeback with granular governance.
OSDK 2.0's linear scaling with Ontology shape (not contents) is a critical architectural property enabling large-scale Ontology adoption.
Strong type-safety is achieved by generating code from the specific subset of the Ontology relevant to each application, not the entire Ontology.
Security is built into the OSDK architecture: tokens are scoped to specific ontological entities, layered on top of user-level permissions.
Centralized Ontology maintenance means application developers focus on building, not maintaining the data foundation.
The OSDK supports four language ecosystems (TypeScript, Python, Java, OpenAPI), making the Ontology accessible across the development landscape.

title: GitHub - palantir/osdk-ts: Typescript related OSDK libraries

extractedEntities.name: osdk-ts
extractedEntities.description: The official open-source TypeScript OSDK monorepo hosted on GitHub, containing multiple packages for interacting with Palantir's Ontology via TypeScript.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/client
extractedEntities.description: The core OSDK client package for TypeScript. Used to create an authenticated client that provides typed access to Ontology entities.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/oauth
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK OAuth package providing createPublicOauthClient and createConfidentialOauthClient for authentication against Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/api
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK API types package defining the core type interfaces used by the generated SDK.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/foundry-sdk-generator
extractedEntities.description: The code generator that produces type-safe TypeScript bindings from Ontology definitions in Developer Console.

extractedEntities.name: foundry-platform-typescript
extractedEntities.description: A standalone package providing direct Foundry API access without needing to generate ontology-specific SDKs.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/create-app
extractedEntities.description: CLI tool for quickly bootstrapping a frontend application using the OSDK.

extractedEntities.name: Public OAuth Client
extractedEntities.description: OAuth client type for browser/frontend applications requiring client ID, Foundry URL, and redirect URL. No client secret needed.

extractedEntities.name: Confidential OAuth Client
extractedEntities.description: OAuth client type for server-side/backend applications requiring client ID, client secret, and Foundry URL. Used with service users.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application used to create OSDK applications and generate SDKs, providing the client ID and configuration needed for OAuth.

keyInsights: The TypeScript OSDK is a mature open-source monorepo with 2,844+ commits and active maintenance, signaling Palantir's commitment to external developer ecosystem.
Two distinct OAuth flows (public and confidential) support both browser-based and server-side application architectures.
foundry-platform-typescript provides a no-code-generation path for developers who want direct Foundry API access without ontology-specific types.
The @osdk/foundry-sdk-generator bridges the gap between Ontology definitions and type-safe TypeScript code.
The monorepo architecture separates concerns: client, auth, API types, code generation, and app scaffolding are all independent packages.

title: The Ontology system - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The system at the heart of Palantir's architecture, designed to represent the complex, interconnected decisions of an enterprise — not simply the data. Enables humans and AI agents to collaborate across operational workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Data-Logic-Action-Security Integration
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology models decisions through the four-fold integration of data (semantic objects/properties/links), logic (business rules, ML, LLMs), action (transactions to edits), and security (role-based, attribute-based access).

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Language
extractedEntities.description: The semantic layer of the Ontology system comprising types (Object Types, Properties, Links), kinetic actions, and logic. Defines the vocabulary for modeling enterprise decisions.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Engine
extractedEntities.description: The read/write architecture of the Ontology system providing SQL queries, real-time subscriptions, atomic transactions, batch mutations, and streaming capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Toolchain
extractedEntities.description: The development and management tools for building, maintaining, and evolving the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Semantic types representing real-world entities (nouns) that unify disparate data sources into coherent objects with properties and links.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attributes that describe Object Types, forming the adjectives in the Ontology's semantic vocabulary.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Defined relationships connecting Object Types in a web of semantic connections.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Kinetic operations (verbs) that model the full range of actions from simple transactions to complex multi-step updates written back to operational systems in real time.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Security Model
extractedEntities.description: Security woven into data, logic, and actions. Different scopes of access are relevant for different team members (e.g., production teams see global telemetry, warehouse associates have regional restrictions).

extractedEntities.name: Airline Flight Operations
extractedEntities.description: An airline models flights, aircraft, crew manifests, scheduling optimizers into their ontology to power day-of operations and longer-range planning.

extractedEntities.name: Hospital System Operations
extractedEntities.description: A hospital models patients, nurse schedules, medical supplies, bed capacities for real-time patient lifecycle management.

extractedEntities.name: Medical Manufacturing
extractedEntities.description: A medical manufacturer models plants, work orders, customer details, shipments, with differentiated access for production teams, warehouse associates, and supply chain analysts.

keyInsights: The Ontology is explicitly designed to model decisions, not data — this is the core architectural distinction from traditional data platforms.
The four-fold integration of data, logic, action, and security is the fundamental design principle of the Ontology system.
Semantics (nouns) must be paired with kinetics (verbs) to model decisions — Object Types alone are insufficient without Action Types.
The Ontology system is decomposed into three major subsystems: Language (what you model), Engine (how it executes), and Toolchain (how you build it).
Security is not a bolt-on concern but is woven into every layer — data, logic, and actions each have security implications.
Logic underlying actions can range from simple business rules to LLM-driven functions to complex multi-step orchestrations, reflecting real-world decision diversity.

title: Overview and getting started - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Metadata Service
extractedEntities.description: Overarching service (OMS) that defines the set of ontological entities that exist — including metadata for object types, link types, action types, and more.

extractedEntities.name: Object Set Service
extractedEntities.description: Service (OSS) responsible for serving reads from the Ontology, enabling searching, filtering, aggregating, and loading of objects.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Next-generation canonical data store for backing the Ontology. Supports higher-scale Search Arounds (100K+ objects) and accurate aggregations via Spark-based query layer.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's legacy Ontology backend component, predecessor to Object Storage V2.

extractedEntities.name: Object Data Funnel
extractedEntities.description: Service responsible for indexing data from datasources into the Ontology object databases.

extractedEntities.name: Functions on Objects
extractedEntities.description: Backend service that executes Functions in the context of Ontology objects.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: Lists of real-world entities saved for future reference and use across Foundry applications. Saved as resources for easy sharing and application integration.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose schema, datasource, and metadata are managed by the Ontology backend services.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attributes of Object Types stored and indexed by object databases for querying and filtering.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types whose metadata is defined in OMS and whose traversals are served by OSS.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Structured, controlled modifications to object data orchestrated through the Ontology backend.

extractedEntities.name: Microservices Architecture
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry Ontology backend uses a microservices architecture where multiple services collectively handle datasource management, querying, and write orchestration.

extractedEntities.name: Search Around
extractedEntities.description: A query operation traversing Link Types between objects. Object Storage V2 supports scales of 100K+ objects for Search Arounds.

keyInsights: The Ontology backend is a composition of specialized microservices: OMS for metadata, OSS for reads, Object Storage for persistence, Data Funnel for indexing, and Functions on Objects for computation.
The Ontology Metadata Service (OMS) is the authoritative source of truth for what ontological entities exist and their schemas.
Object Set Service (OSS) is the single read path for all Ontology queries — it mediates between applications and the underlying object databases.
Object Storage V2 represents a generational leap: Spark-based query layer enables 100K+ object Search Arounds and accurate aggregations that were limited in V1.
The Ontology serves as a digital twin containing both semantic elements (objects, properties, links) and kinetic elements (actions, functions, dynamic security).
ObjectSets are first-class resources — saved lists of entities that can be shared and reused across applications.

title: Ontology Manager - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: The primary Palantir application (also called OMA) for building and maintaining the Ontology. Provides UI for creating object types, defining action types, connecting data, and investigating data updates.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology entity created and configured in Ontology Manager with metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and primary key configuration.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attributes of Object Types configured through the Property editor view in Ontology Manager. Includes mapping to backing datasource columns.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types visualized and configured through the link type graph in Ontology Manager.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology actions configured through a dedicated view in Ontology Manager, defining structured modifications to objects.

extractedEntities.name: Function Type
extractedEntities.description: Functions configured and viewed through a dedicated function type view in Ontology Manager.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A Property that is shared across multiple Object Types, configured in Ontology Manager for consistent semantic representation.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Branch
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Manager supports branching for creating and testing Ontology changes in isolation before merging, analogous to Git branches.

extractedEntities.name: Property Mapping
extractedEntities.description: The configuration that maps Object Type properties to columns in a backing datasource, defining how raw data becomes semantic Ontology properties.

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: The unique identifier configuration for an Object Type, ensuring each object instance is uniquely addressable.

keyInsights: Ontology Manager is the single pane of glass for all Ontology configuration — object types, properties, links, actions, and functions are all managed here.
Object Type creation follows a guided workflow: metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and primary key configuration.
Ontology branching enables safe, isolated changes to the Ontology schema before deployment — critical for production Ontology governance.
Shared Properties enable consistent semantic representation across multiple Object Types, promoting Ontology reuse.
The Discover view provides personalized navigation with favorites, recent views, and group-based organization for large Ontologies.
Property mapping is the bridge between raw data (datasource columns) and semantic Ontology attributes (properties).

title: Ontology-Oriented Software Development - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Oriented Development
extractedEntities.description: A new software development paradigm where programmers abstract away internal implementation details and interact with business concepts. The OSDK functions as a higher-level abstraction for authoring business logic over harmonized critical business concepts.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Software Development Kit that enables ontology-oriented development by providing a toolkit for building APIs in the language of the business.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The technology that centralizes business knowledge and encapsulates it within a shared system, enabling defragmentation of the enterprise by integrating isolated components into a holistic system.

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise Defragmentation
extractedEntities.description: The architectural pattern of integrating isolated software components into a holistic system through the Ontology, replacing the fragmented vendor-driven enterprise architecture.

extractedEntities.name: Decision-Centric Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Software architecture centered on modeling and executing enterprise decisions rather than optimizing individual data components.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Business entities modeled in the Ontology (e.g., Aircraft, Flights, Mechanics) that developers interact with directly rather than through low-level data access.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Business operations modeled in the Ontology (e.g., reassigning mechanics, rerouting flights) that are invoked as type-safe functions in the OSDK.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction enabling polymorphic access to Object Types, allowing applications to work with shared contracts across different entity types.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Code-based logic operating on Ontology entities that is shared and reusable across applications through the OSDK.

extractedEntities.name: Airline Maintenance Optimization
extractedEntities.description: An airline uses the Ontology to model aircraft, flights, mechanics, and maintenance schedules, enabling real-time reassignment of mechanics when warning lights trigger during routes.

keyInsights: Ontology-oriented development is positioned as a new paradigm alongside object-oriented and functional programming — abstracting business concepts rather than data structures.
The core thesis: enterprise software failure is not a technology problem but an architecture problem — optimizing components in isolation degrades system-level performance.
The OSDK makes the marginal cost of bespoke enterprise software development approach zero by reusing shared Ontology integration work.
Knowledge compounds in the Ontology: each new application benefits from all previous integration work, creating a flywheel effect.
The Ontology aligns business users and engineers around shared business representation rather than underlying component architectures.
Interfaces enable polymorphic programming against the Ontology, allowing code to work across different Object Types sharing a common contract.

title: Ontology: Finding meaning in data - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A systematic mapping of data to meaningful semantic concepts. Exists outside the data itself to establish a framework that empowers data integration, application building, and user collaboration. Functions regardless of the specific data present.

extractedEntities.name: Three Levels of Abstraction
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology operates at three levels: (1) Class definitions (abstract object type schemas), (2) Instantiated objects (real data mapped to classes), and (3) Operational use (linked, created, deleted, property changes).

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Class definitions in the Ontology representing the nouns of an organization (e.g., Accounts, Transactions, Financial Products for a bank).

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Defined attributes that describe Object Type classes, forming the adjectives in the Ontology's semantic vocabulary.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Defined relationships connecting object classes in a web, enabling navigation between related entities.

extractedEntities.name: Instantiated Object
extractedEntities.description: A concrete representation of data mapped into a defined Object Type class. Can be created, deleted, linked, unlinked, and have properties changed.

extractedEntities.name: Data Agnosticism
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology is data-agnostic: while data may inform how the Ontology is structured, the Ontology itself functions regardless of the specific data present in the ecosystem.

extractedEntities.name: Banking Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A bank models Accounts, Transactions, and Financial Products as Object Types in the Ontology, with defined properties and relationships between them.

extractedEntities.name: Nouns-Verbs-Adjectives Model
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology defines the nouns (Object Types), verbs (Actions/Links), and adjectives (Properties) of an organization's semantic vocabulary.

keyInsights: Data does not have inherent meaning — meaning is layered onto data by users through the Ontology, making Ontology design a fundamentally human activity.
The Ontology exists outside the data itself, functioning as a schema-level framework that is independent of specific data contents.
Data agnosticism is a key design principle: the Ontology should function regardless of what data is present, enabling it to evolve independently of data sources.
Three levels of abstraction (class definitions, instantiated objects, operational use) mirror the class/instance/operation pattern in object-oriented programming.
The Nouns-Verbs-Adjectives model provides an intuitive mental framework for Ontology design that maps directly to natural language thinking about business domains.
A functional data ecosystem must incorporate Ontology concepts to be scalable and sustainable — without semantic meaning, data remains inert.

title: Connecting AI to Decisions with the Palantir Ontology - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Decision-centric software architecture integrating data, logic, and action into a scalable, dynamic, collaborative foundation reflecting real-time organizational conditions.

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform that enables LLMs and AI agents to query and reason over the Ontology using natural language, safely leveraging AI in enterprise operations.

extractedEntities.name: Decision-Centric Model
extractedEntities.description: Every decision comprises three elements: data (information used to decide), logic (process of evaluating), and action (execution of the decision). The Ontology integrates all three.

extractedEntities.name: Tools Paradigm
extractedEntities.description: Rather than fine-tuning LLMs on enterprise data, AIP provides LLMs with tools (Ontology queries, actions, functions) that the AI calls to interact with the real world through the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Semantic types that integrate all modalities of data (ERPs, IoT, unstructured, geospatial) into a full-scale representation surfaced in the language of the enterprise.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Kinetic operations ranging from simple transactions to multi-step orchestrations. The Ontology captures the full lifecycle including guardrails, side effects, and downstream implications.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Logic elements that can be simple business rules, ML models, LLM-driven functions, or complex multi-step orchestrations. Composed and evolved over time.

extractedEntities.name: Decision Data
extractedEntities.description: Data generated by end users as decisions are made, including context, options evaluated, and downstream implications. Distinct from traditional enterprise data.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Governance
extractedEntities.description: Security model woven into the Ontology ensuring that both human users and AI agents operate within defined access controls, guardrails, and audit trails.

extractedEntities.name: Real-time Subscriptions
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Engine provides real-time subscription capabilities for live updates to Ontology state changes.

extractedEntities.name: Atomic Transactions
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Engine supports atomic transactions ensuring consistency when multiple objects are modified simultaneously.

keyInsights: The Ontology is designed to represent decisions, not data — this is the fundamental architectural distinction enabling AI integration.
AIP uses a tools paradigm rather than fine-tuning: LLMs call Ontology tools (queries, actions, functions) rather than being trained on enterprise data.
Decision Data is a novel concept: the context, options, and implications generated during decision-making become first-class data in the Ontology, enabling learning loops.
The three elements of any decision (data, logic, action) map directly to the Ontology's three pillars, making the Ontology a natural substrate for AI-driven decisions.
Security governance for AI is the same governance used for humans — the Ontology's access controls, guardrails, and audit trails apply equally to LLM agents.
Real-time capabilities (subscriptions, atomic transactions, streaming) are essential Engine features for operational decision-making where latency matters.

title: Building with Palantir AIP: The Ontology Software Development Kit - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Software Development Kit that generates a custom SDK for a specific business ontology, enabling type-safe interaction with business objects from external development environments.

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform within which the OSDK enables building external applications that leverage LLMs integrated with the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Portal for developing applications that leverage the Ontology, used to create OSDK applications with scoped access to specific Ontology entities.

extractedEntities.name: SDK-of-Your-Business
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK generates an SDK that is an SDK of the business itself, using the language and concepts of the business domain, not generic API endpoints.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Business entities (e.g., Distribution Centers, Satellites, Customer Orders) that become type-safe objects in the generated OSDK code.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Business operations (e.g., updating orders, tasking satellites) exposed as type-safe functions in the OSDK for writeback to the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Logic elements that can be invoked through the OSDK, including LLM-driven functions for satellite selection.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Token Scoping
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK application token is scoped to only the ontological entities the application needs, providing least-privilege access control.

extractedEntities.name: Satellite Imagery Tasking
extractedEntities.description: Titan Industries extends their Ontology to include satellite tasking, using LLMs through the OSDK to find the right satellite to capture imagery of a damaged distribution center.

extractedEntities.name: React OSDK Integration
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK provides TypeScript bindings specifically optimized for React applications, enabling type-safe frontend development against the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Automatic Documentation Generation
extractedEntities.description: Developer Console automatically generates API documentation from Ontology metadata, keeping docs in sync with the Ontology schema.

keyInsights: The OSDK generates an SDK of your business — not generic API client code, but domain-specific typed interfaces reflecting actual business entities.
Developer Console acts as the bridge between Ontology definitions and external application development, handling SDK generation and token scoping.
The OSDK enables a composable ecosystem: new applications leverage all existing Ontology integration work, and new components onboard quickly.
LLM integration through the OSDK demonstrates the tools paradigm in practice: AI selects satellites by reasoning over typed Ontology objects.
Automatic documentation generation from Ontology metadata ensures that OSDK documentation stays in sync without manual maintenance.
Security is least-privilege by default: OSDK tokens are scoped to specific ontological entities, not broad platform access.

title: Pipeline Builder - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's primary application for data integration, enabling point-and-click pipeline building with a next-generation transformation backend acting as intermediary between logic creation and execution.

extractedEntities.name: Expressions
extractedEntities.description: Column-level transformations in Pipeline Builder that operate on individual values within a dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Transforms
extractedEntities.description: Table-level operations in Pipeline Builder that modify dataset structure (joins, filters, aggregations, unions).

extractedEntities.name: User-Defined Functions (UDFs)
extractedEntities.description: Custom code functions in Pipeline Builder for computations that built-in expressions and transforms do not cover.

extractedEntities.name: Apache Spark
extractedEntities.description: Distributed compute engine leveraged by Pipeline Builder for batch data transformation processing.

extractedEntities.name: Apache Flink
extractedEntities.description: Stream processing engine leveraged by Pipeline Builder for real-time streaming pipeline execution.

extractedEntities.name: Transformation Backend
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder's next-generation backend that acts as a middle layer between logic creation and execution, writing transform code, performing integrity checks, and identifying refactoring errors.

extractedEntities.name: Compute-Agnostic Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder abstracts the execution engine: the same pipeline logic can execute on Spark, Flink, or other engines without user modification.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entities that Pipeline Builder can output to, feeding the Ontology from transformed data.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Outputs
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder can output to datasets, objects, streams, time-series, and exports — connecting raw data to the Ontology and beyond.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipelines
extractedEntities.description: Real-time latency pipeline execution capability in Pipeline Builder, enabling continuous data processing.

keyInsights: Pipeline Builder's transformation backend acts as an intelligent intermediary: it writes code, checks integrity, and identifies errors before execution — not just a visual wrapper.
The compute-agnostic architecture means Pipeline Builder logic is portable across Spark, Flink, and other engines without rewriting.
Pipeline Builder bridges the gap between no-code users and code-first engineers through a layered approach: point-and-click UI, expressions, transforms, and UDFs.
Outputs include objects (feeding the Ontology), streams, time-series, and exports — making Pipeline Builder a gateway from raw data to semantic Ontology.
Type-safe functions flag errors immediately rather than at build time, reducing the feedback loop for pipeline authors.
Automatic build path pruning avoids unnecessary computation by trimming disconnected transform paths.

title: Overview - Data Integration - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's highly configurable data connectivity and integration toolset extending far beyond typical ETL/ELT solutions. Designed to reduce the cost of data integration over time through capabilities that act as a force multiplier.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for fast, flexible, and scalable delivery of data pipelines with integrated robustness and security.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection Framework
extractedEntities.description: Extensible framework establishing connections with all types of source systems (structured, unstructured, semi-structured) using batch, micro-batch, or streaming approaches.

extractedEntities.name: Compute-Agnostic Build Framework
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's Build framework provides fully integrated security and data lineage while enabling mixing and matching of third-party compute runtimes.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Full lineage tracking of data versions throughout the integration pipeline, from source through transformation to Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Health Checks
extractedEntities.description: Rigorous release process for production pipelines including health checks guaranteeing only fully compliant data is deployed.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The end destination for integrated data: raw data is connected, transformed, and ultimately mapped into Object Types in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Security
extractedEntities.description: Granular security for collaborative management of data extraction, with branching of data sync configurations and integrated governance.

keyInsights: Foundry data integration is explicitly designed to reduce cost over time — a force multiplier philosophy rather than one-time ETL.
The compute-agnostic Build framework is a foundational architectural pattern: security and lineage are integrated regardless of which compute engine runs.
The data integration layer spans four capabilities: connecting, transforming, managing pipelines, and quality assurance — each with dedicated tooling.
Pipeline health checks enforce that only compliant data reaches production, providing a quality gate before Ontology ingestion.
Data lineage from source to Ontology is a first-class capability, enabling full auditability of how raw data becomes semantic objects.

title: Data Connection - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application for synchronizing data from external systems into Foundry. Supports batch, streaming, media, CDC syncs, virtual tables, and manages source credentials.

extractedEntities.name: Raw Data Ingestion
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection philosophy that data should be ingested as-is from its most raw source with no external preprocessing, making the Foundry pipeline the single source of all transformations.

extractedEntities.name: Batch Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection sync mode for periodic bulk data transfer from external systems.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection sync mode for continuous real-time data ingestion from external systems.

extractedEntities.name: CDC Sync
extractedEntities.description: Change Data Capture sync mode that captures only incremental changes from source systems.

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Tables
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection capability enabling query-time access to external data without copying it into Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Media Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection sync mode for ingesting unstructured media files (images, video, PDFs) from external systems.

extractedEntities.name: Webhook Writeback
extractedEntities.description: Outbound Data Connection capability enabling writeback to external systems via webhooks.

extractedEntities.name: Robustness Through Simplicity
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection uses simple functions (filesystem, database syncs) to pull data in small batches with low-complexity queries, combined with automatic retries and health monitoring.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The downstream destination for data synchronized through Data Connection, after transformation in the pipeline layer.

keyInsights: Data Connection deliberately ingests data as-is with no preprocessing, making Foundry the single source of truth for all transformations.
Five sync modes (batch, streaming, media, CDC, virtual tables) cover the full spectrum of data ingestion patterns.
Virtual Tables enable query-time federation — accessing external data without copying it into Foundry, useful for compliance or latency requirements.
Robustness is achieved through simplicity: small batches, low-complexity queries, automatic retries, and health monitoring rather than complex resilience patterns.
Data Connection also supports outbound connections via webhooks and data exports, making it bidirectional not just ingest-only.
Extensibility is a core design principle: new source systems can often reuse existing plugins with minor changes.

title: On dataset versioning in Palantir Foundry - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The fundamental data representation in Foundry. Datasets use immutable versions (analogous to Git commits) enabling concurrent read/write access without conflicts.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that replaces the current view entirely with a new set of files. The basis of batch pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that adds new files without modifying existing ones. The basis of incremental pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: UPDATE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that modifies specific existing files within the dataset view.

extractedEntities.name: DELETE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that removes specific files from the dataset view.

extractedEntities.name: Git-for-Data
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's dataset versioning model parallels Git: transactions are analogous to commits, dataset branches parallel Git branches, enabling versioning over both logic and data simultaneously.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Branching
extractedEntities.description: Datasets support branching that isolates changes, enabling concurrent development on different versions of the same dataset without interference.

extractedEntities.name: Immutable Versions
extractedEntities.description: Each committed dataset transaction creates an immutable version, ensuring atomic reads and preventing mixed-version inconsistencies.

extractedEntities.name: Dual Branching
extractedEntities.description: Foundry combines dataset branching (data versioning) with Git branching (logic versioning) to support versioning over both data and transformation code simultaneously.

keyInsights: Dataset versioning through immutable transactions solves the fundamental problem of concurrent read/write access in collaborative data engineering.
Four transaction types (SNAPSHOT, APPEND, UPDATE, DELETE) provide precise control over how datasets evolve, analogous to different Git operations.
Dataset branching enables sandbox development: data engineers can experiment on isolated branches without affecting production data or other engineers' work.
Dual branching (data + logic) is Foundry's unique capability: versioning both the transformation code and the data it produces simultaneously.
Without versioning, exported data suffers from non-deterministic mixed versions during concurrent access — a subtle but critical correctness issue.
APPEND transactions are the foundation of incremental pipelines, processing only new data rather than recomputing entire datasets.

title: Core Concepts - Datasets - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The most essential representation of data in Foundry, from when it lands through when it is mapped into the Ontology. A wrapper around a collection of files in a backing file system with integrated permission, schema, and version control.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset View
extractedEntities.description: The current state of a dataset as seen by users — the result of all committed transactions up to the latest version.

extractedEntities.name: Transaction Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: A dataset transaction follows a lifecycle: OPEN (files can be written), COMMITTED (changes visible in view), or ABORTED (changes ignored).

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Replaces the entire current dataset view with a new set of files. The basis of batch pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Adds new files to the current view without modifying existing files. Cannot overwrite existing files. The basis of incremental pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: UPDATE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Modifies specific existing files within the dataset. Supports row-level operations.

extractedEntities.name: DELETE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Removes specific files from the dataset view.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Schema
extractedEntities.description: Metadata describing the columns of structured (tabular) datasets, stored alongside the dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology entity that datasets are ultimately mapped into, with datasets providing the backing data for Object Type properties.

extractedEntities.name: Git-for-Data
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transactions are analogous to Git commits — atomic, immutable changes that together form the version history of a dataset.

keyInsights: Datasets are the fundamental abstraction in Foundry — everything from landing to Ontology mapping flows through datasets.
A dataset is fundamentally a wrapper around files, not a database table — this enables support for structured, unstructured, and semi-structured data.
The transaction model (OPEN, COMMITTED, ABORTED) provides atomicity guarantees: either all changes apply or none do.
Four transaction types map to distinct pipeline patterns: SNAPSHOT for batch, APPEND for incremental, UPDATE for row-level modifications, DELETE for removals.
Datasets bridge the gap from raw data to Ontology: data lands as files, gets structured through schemas, and is ultimately mapped into Object Types.
Foundry datasets provide integrated permission management, schema management, and version control — capabilities absent from raw file systems.
